attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-year history of difficulty seeing at night and bumping into objects in dim lighting. He reports that his vision seems to be getting worse bilaterally, particularly when walking at dusk. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His parent and older sibling have similar visual symptoms.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Visual acuity is 6/6 in both eyes. Confrontation visual field testing reveals constricted peripheral fields bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows bilateral bone spicule pigmentation in the mid-peripheral retina, attenuated retinal vessels, and waxy pallor of the optic discs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of retinitis pigmentosa: night blindness (nyctalopia), progressive bilateral peripheral visual field loss, and characteristic fundoscopic findings of bone spicule pigmentation. The positive family history suggests an inherited pattern, which is common in retinitis pigmentosa though may be autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, X-linked or even sporadic depending on the mutation. Patients are typically legally blind by by age 40.Patients would be investigated including with full-field electroretinogram and genetic testing for diagnosis.

Retinitis pigmentosa is a group of inherited retinal disorders characterised by progressive degeneration of photoreceptors, primarily affecting rods before cones. This explains why night vision and peripheral vision (rod-mediated) are affected before central vision. The characteristic bone spicule pigmentation results from migration of retinal pigment epithelium into the neural retina following photoreceptor death.

Age-related macular degeneration typically affects older adults and primarily involves central vision rather than peripheral vision. It would not cause nyctalopia, and the fundoscopic findings would show drusen or geographic atrophy in the macula.

Central serous retinopathy typically affects young to middle-aged adults and presents with central vision distortion. It would show a serous detachment of the neurosensory retina on examination, not bone spicule pigmentation.

Diabetic retinopathy requires a history of diabetes mellitus and would show microaneurysms, dot-and-blot haemorrhages, and cotton wool spots on fundoscopy. The pattern of visual loss would not typically begin with nyctalopia.

Optic neuritis typically presents with acute or subacute vision loss and pain with eye movement. The fundoscopic appearance would show optic disc swelling or pallor without retinal pigmentary changes.
learningPoints: Retinitis pigmentosa classically presents with night blindness and progressive peripheral visual field loss.
Bone spicule pigmentation on fundoscopy is characteristic of retinitis pigmentosa.
Family history is important as retinitis pigmentosa can be inherited in autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or X-linked patterns.
references: Kamde SP, Anjankar A. Retinitis Pigmentosa: Pathogenesis, Diagnostic Findings, and Treatment. Cureus. 2023 Oct 30;15(10).  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10686897/
Hsu J et al. Retinitis Pigmentosa. American Academy of Ophthalmology.  2024.  Available at: https://eyewiki.org/Retinitis_Pigmentosa
